• `repo/NemoClaw/docs/about/how-it-works.md`

───

Repo-level architecture comparison

1. OpenClaw is a platform monolith; NemoClaw is a wrapper/orchestrator

OpenClaw

OpenClaw’s repo is broad and product-complete.

Its `src/` includes major domains like:

• `src/gateway`
• `src/agents`
• `src/channels`
• `src/sessions`
• `src/hooks`
• `src/plugins`
• `src/plugin-sdk`
• `src/providers`
• `src/routing`

• `src/cron`
• `src/browser`
• `src/web`
• `src/tui`
• `src/acp`
• `src/node-host`

It also has:

• many extensions in `extensions/`
• mobile/desktop apps
• large docs surface
• UI and operational packaging

Interpretation

OpenClaw is the **system of record** for:

• agent runtime
• control plane
• transport/channel connectivity
• extension model
• session semantics
• event semantics
• operator-facing behavior

───

NemoClaw

NemoClaw is much smaller and split in two:

A. TypeScript plugin

Under:

• `nemoclaw/src/`

This registers commands under:

• `openclaw nemoclaw ...`

The plugin is thin and mostly does:

• resolve blueprint
• verify blueprint
• launch/migrate/connect/status/logs
• shell out to OpenShell / blueprint runner

B. Python blueprint

Under:

• `nemoclaw-blueprint/`

This handles:

• planning
• apply
• sandbox orchestration
• policy
• inference setup

Interpretation

NemoClaw is **not its own full agent platform**.
It is an **OpenClaw plugin + deployment blueprint** that moves OpenClaw into a controlled OpenShell sandbox.

That’s a huge distinction.

───

What is architecturally the same

These are the parts where a shared abstraction still makes sense.

1. Both ultimately run OpenClaw agent workloads

NemoClaw’s README is explicit:

• “NVIDIA NemoClaw: OpenClaw Plugin for OpenShell”
• “run OpenClaw inside OpenShell”
• “OpenClaw runs with the NemoClaw plugin pre-installed”

So in both cases the operator is still aiming to run:

• OpenClaw agent behavior

• OpenClaw workflows
• OpenClaw CLI/TUI patterns

That means ClawDash portability is still valid as a goal.

───

2. Both care about lifecycle operations

Both have operational concerns like:

• launch
• status
• logs
• connect
• health
• configuration

OpenClaw exposes these more natively as part of its platform.
NemoClaw wraps them around:

• sandbox state
• blueprint run state
• inference provider status

So a normalized lifecycle contract is still useful.

───

3. Both involve tools, policies, and runtime execution

OpenClaw has:

• tools
• policies

• sessions
• hooks
• channels
• model routing

NemoClaw adds:

• filesystem policy
• network policy
• process restrictions
• inference routing through OpenShell

So both live in the broader world of:

• controlled execution
• tool access
• policy boundaries

• runtime state

───

What is different

This is where ClawDapter needs to be careful.

1. OpenClaw is the runtime platform; NemoClaw is a deployment mode around it

This is the biggest point.

OpenClaw

The runtime itself owns:

• gateway

• sessions
• channels
• tools
• event streams
• plugin system
• control plane

NemoClaw

The repo shows it owns:

• plugin command wiring
• blueprint resolution/verification
• OpenShell orchestration
• sandbox policy
• inference provider routing
• migration into sandbox

Meaning

NemoClaw is not “another independent runtime API” in the same way OpenClaw is.
It is more like:

• **OpenClaw-on-OpenShell**
• a constrained/sandboxed operating mode

That means ClawDapter may need to distinguish between:

• **runtime platform**
and
• **execution environment**

───

2. OpenClaw owns interaction surfaces; NemoClaw mostly owns containment

OpenClaw includes:

• WhatsApp
• Telegram
• Slack
• Discord
• Signal
• iMessage
• WebChat
• CLI/TUI
• WS gateway
• nodes

NemoClaw’s repo focuses on:

• sandbox creation
• connect to sandbox shell

• status
• logs
• policy approvals via OpenShell
• inference switching

Meaning

If ClawDash expects every backend to expose OpenClaw-like interaction semantics, that is wrong.

NemoClaw is not adding a new multi-channel platform.
It is adding a **secure envelope** around OpenClaw.

───

3. OpenClaw’s core abstraction is sessions/gateway; NemoClaw’s is sandbox/blueprint/policy

OpenClaw docs are full of:

• gateway WS protocol
• sessions
• events
• presence
• queueing
• tool execution
• channel routing

NemoClaw docs are full of:

• plugin
• blueprint
• OpenShell sandbox
• policy files
• inference profiles
• migration
• approval of network requests

Meaning

The center of gravity is different.

OpenClaw asks:

• how does the agent behave?

NemoClaw asks:

• under what **execution constraints** does OpenClaw behave?

───

4. OpenClaw has broad extensibility; NemoClaw is intentionally narrow

OpenClaw has:

• `extensions/`
• `plugin-sdk`
• many plugin exports
• broad surface area

NemoClaw is intentionally compact:

• thin plugin
• blueprint runner
• policy presets
• operational commands

Meaning

NemoClaw is currently not a general extension platform in the same sense.
It’s a targeted integration product.

───

NemoClaw-specific architecture findings

These are the most concrete things from the repo.

1. NemoClaw is officially a plugin

From:

• `nemoclaw/openclaw.plugin.json`

It defines config like:

• `blueprintVersion`
• `blueprintRegistry`
• `sandboxName`

• `inferenceProvider`

That is plugin-like configuration, not a standalone runtime contract.

───

2. The plugin is deliberately thin

From:

• `nemoclaw/src/commands/launch.ts`

It mostly:

• checks existing OpenClaw installation
• resolves blueprint
• verifies digest

• checks compatibility
• runs plan/apply through the blueprint
• saves state
• points users to connect/status/OpenShell

That means the “business logic” is not in the plugin.
It is in the **blueprint + OpenShell**.

───

3. The blueprint defines versioned compatibility

From:

• `nemoclaw-blueprint/blueprint.yaml`

It explicitly declares:

• `min_openshell_version`
• `min_openclaw_version`
• profiles:
  • `default`
  • `ncp`
  • `nim-local`
  • `vllm`

This is important because it means NemoClaw is explicitly version-gated against OpenClaw.

ClawDapter implication

Version compatibility should be a first-class capability/health concern.

───

4. NemoClaw’s strongest differentiator is policy-controlled isolation

From:

• `nemoclaw-blueprint/policies/openclaw-sandbox.yaml`

It defines:

• filesystem read-only/read-write zones
• Landlock compatibility
• process user/group
• endpoint-level network policies
• binary-specific access rules
• explicit allowed outbound hosts

Examples allowed:

• NVIDIA inference endpoints
• GitHub

• OpenClaw docs/API/ClawHub
• npm registry
• Telegram API

ClawDapter implication

This is not just “runtime metadata.”
It affects:

• what tools work
• what workflows are possible
• what channels are reachable
• whether integrations fail
• whether operator approval is needed

───

5. NemoClaw supports migration of existing OpenClaw into sandbox

From:

• `nemoclaw/src/commands/migrate.ts`

It can:

• detect host OpenClaw state
• package state/config/external roots
• copy them into sandbox
• verify rewritten paths
• preserve rollback snapshot

ClawDapter implication

NemoClaw has lifecycle/migration concerns OpenClaw itself does not expose the same way.

That matters operationally, but it probably belongs in ClawTomation / deployment tooling more than in ClawDash UX.

───

Areas we need to worry about

1. Treating NemoClaw as a separate “agent runtime API”

This is the top risk.

If we model this as:

• OpenClawAdapter
• NemoClawAdapter

with the assumption that both are equivalent top-level runtimes,
we may build the wrong abstraction.

A more correct model may be:

• **OpenClaw platform adapter**
• **Execution environment adapter or policy profile**
  • native OpenClaw
  • OpenClaw-in-NemoClaw/OpenShell
  • future alternatives

If we don’t separate those, we may mix:

• runtime semantics
with
• sandbox/deployment semantics

───

2. Capability differences caused by policy, not by code

In NemoClaw, a feature may “exist” in OpenClaw but be blocked by:

• egress policy
• filesystem policy
• inference routing setup
• missing provider approval
• sandbox constraints

So capability is dynamic.

Example

OpenClaw may support:

• browser tool
• GitHub access
• plugin installs

• outbound web fetches

But inside NemoClaw those may be:

• allowed
• blocked
• approval-gated
• profile-dependent

ClawDapter implication

Capability detection must be runtime-context-aware, not just static.

───

3. Session semantics are stable in OpenClaw but environment-dependent in NemoClaw

OpenClaw’s session model is clear and first-class.

In NemoClaw, the session model is still OpenClaw’s, but the environment around it may affect:

• persistence paths
• install state
• plugin availability
• network-dependent features
• migration health

ClawDapter implication

Canonical execution must separate:

• job/session state
from
• environment health / sandbox health

───

4. Operator UX can drift if ClawDash leaks sandbox details everywhere

If ClawDash starts exposing every OpenShell/NemoClaw detail directly in core flows, the UI becomes backend-specific.

Recommendation

Keep the normalized UX centered on:

• job status
• runtime/environment
• capability warnings
• policy restrictions
• final result

Then provide expanded details for:

• sandbox health
• network approval backlog
• inference profile
• migration status

as advanced panels, not core workflow blockers.

───

5. Routing ownership belongs in ClawTomation

This gets even more true after repo inspection.

Because choosing “NemoClaw” may imply:

• sandbox requirement
• local/cloud policy

• inference profile
• migration readiness
• network policy compatibility

That is orchestration logic, not just UI selection.

So ClawTomation should likely own:

• when to route into OpenClaw-native
• when to route into OpenClaw-via-NemoClaw
• when to reject or warn because the environment cannot satisfy policy/capability needs

───

What I think ClawDapter should model now

Based on the repos, I would not stop at “runtime adapter.”

I would define two layers.

Layer 1: Canonical execution contract

For ClawDash / ClawTomation:

• `submitJob`
• `getExecutionStatus`
• `streamExecutionEvents`
• `cancelExecution`
• `getResult`
• `getCapabilities`

Layer 2: Execution environment metadata

For backend/environment context:

• `platform`: `openclaw`
• `environment`: `native | nemoclaw-openshell | future`
• `sandboxed`: boolean
• `policyMode`
• `inferenceProfile`
• `egressMode`
• `migrationState`
• `compatibilityState`

That separation will save you pain later.

───

Best current comparison in one table

```
```

```
| Area                   | OpenClaw                    | NemoClaw                                      |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Product role           | Full agent/gateway platform | OpenClaw plugin + sandbox orchestration layer |
| Repo size/scope        | Large monorepo              | Small integration repo                        |
| Core language          | TypeScript-heavy            | TypeScript plugin + Python blueprint          |
| Owns channels          | Yes                         | No, inherits via OpenClaw                     |
| Owns agent runtime     | Yes                         | Indirectly, by running OpenClaw in OpenShell  |
| Owns session model     | Yes                         | Uses OpenClaw’s session model                 |
| Owns sandbox/policy    | Some runtime/tool policy    | Yes, strongly                                 |
| Owns inference routing | Model/provider config       | Yes via OpenShell profiles                    |
| Operator concern       | Agent behavior/workflows    | Safety, isolation, deployment mode            |
| Architectural type     | Platform runtime            | Secure execution environment for OpenClaw     |
```
───

Bottom line

My strongest conclusion

**OpenClaw and NemoClaw are not the same architectural layer.**

• OpenClaw is the **platform**
• NemoClaw is an **execution/deployment/security mode** for that platform

So if ClawDapter is designed as “one adapter for OpenClaw, one adapter for NemoClaw,” that may be too simplistic.

A better model is likely:

• canonical workflow/execution contract

## Practical reality
For the **near-term implementation**, we are not replacing OpenClaw.

We are:

- standing up a **new OpenClaw server**
- adding **NemoClaw on top of it**
- learning the NemoClaw/OpenShell operating model
- understanding what changes when OpenClaw runs inside that environment

So in the immediate term, the stack is more like:

- **ClawDash** → operator/UI
- **ClawTomation** → orchestration/lifecycle
- **OpenClaw** → core agent platform/server

- **NemoClaw** → secured/sandboxed runtime layer around that OpenClaw deployment

---

## Strategic reality
Even though NemoClaw is being added on top of OpenClaw right now, we **should not** design ClawDash as “OpenClaw + special NemoClaw exceptions.”

We should design an abstraction layer so that, if other agentic systems become viable backends, we can adapt to them too:

- OpenClaw
- OpenClaw + NemoClaw/OpenShell
- Claude Code
- Codex
- future agent runtimes
- other hosted or self-hosted agent systems

That is exactly what **ClawDapter** should be for.

---

# The key design correction

## Don’t model this as only “OpenClaw vs NemoClaw”
That’s too narrow.

Instead, model it as:

### 1. Workflow platform contract
What ClawDash / ClawTomation need from any backend:
- submit work
- monitor work
- stream progress
- fetch results
- cancel/retry
- inspect capabilities

### 2. Backend type
What kind of thing is underneath:
- OpenClaw server
- OpenClaw server running inside NemoClaw/OpenShell
- Claude Code runtime
- Codex runtime
- future agent system

### 3. Environment / execution mode
What constraints or enhancements exist:
- native
- sandboxed
- local-only
- hybrid cloud/local
- policy-gated
- hosted
- approval-gated

- restricted tools/network/filesystem

That lets us support the current plan **and** future-proof it.

---

# Recommended architecture

## Canonical mental model
ClawDash should never care whether the backend is:
- OpenClaw native
- OpenClaw inside NemoClaw
- Codex
- Claude Code

ClawDash should only care about:
- “Can I run this workflow?”

- “What is the status?”
- “What result came back?”
- “What capabilities are available?”

So the adapter layer should sit between ClawDash/ClawTomation and the backend.

---

## Suggested layers

### ClawDash
Owns:
- operator flows
- task creation
- status display
- results/logs UI

### ClawTomation
Owns:
- routing
- lifecycle
- retry/cancel
- escalation
- policy-based dispatch
- follow-up automation

### ClawDapter
Owns:
- canonical backend contract
- adapter implementations
- capability normalization
- event normalization
- environment metadata

### Backend adapters
Examples:
- `OpenClawAdapter`
- `OpenClawNemoClawAdapter`
- `ClaudeCodeAdapter`
- `CodexAdapter`

---

# For your current situation, the most important distinction is this

## OpenClaw native vs OpenClaw inside NemoClaw
Those are likely **not** two entirely separate product integrations.

They may be better represented as:

- same **platform family**: OpenClaw

- different **execution environments**:
  - native
  - NemoClaw/OpenShell sandboxed

This matters because Claude Code and Codex are more likely to be true **different platforms**, while NemoClaw is more like a **secured execution mode around OpenClaw**.

So you may want ClawDapter to support both:

### Platform adapters
- OpenClaw
- Claude Code
- Codex

### Environment profiles
- native
- NemoClaw/OpenShell
- hosted

- sandboxed
- restricted
- local-only

That is cleaner than pretending every difference is the same kind of difference.

---

# What to build first

## 1. Canonical execution contract
Define the core interface all backends must satisfy.

Something like:

- `submitJob(job)`
- `getExecution(executionId)`

- `streamExecutionEvents(executionId)`
- `cancelExecution(executionId)`
- `getResult(executionId)`
- `getCapabilities(target)`

---

## 2. Canonical job schema
This should include more than a prompt.

At minimum:
- `jobId`
- `workflowType`
- `objective`
- `instructions`
- `context`
- `attachments`

- `toolPermissions`
- `constraints`
- `privacyClass`
- `sandboxRequirement`
- `runtimePreference`
- `environmentPreference`
- `metadata`

---

## 3. Capability model
This is critical if you want Codex / Claude Code / OpenClaw / NemoClaw all to fit.

Capabilities should cover:
- persistent sessions
- streaming
- tool use

- tool traces
- file attachments
- sandboxing
- network restrictions
- local model support
- cloud model support
- hybrid routing
- approval-gated actions
- automation hooks
- channel integrations

And each should be reported as:
- supported
- unsupported
- conditional
- unknown

---

## 4. Environment model
Because NemoClaw changes execution conditions, define something like:

- `platform`: `openclaw | claude-code | codex | ...`
- `environment`: `native | nemoclaw-openshell | hosted | sandboxed`
- `policyMode`
- `inferenceMode`
- `networkMode`
- `filesystemMode`

This will help a lot later.

---

# Immediate practical recommendation

For the first real implementation path, I would do this:

## Phase 1
Treat your near-term target as:

- **Platform:** OpenClaw
- **Environment A:** native
- **Environment B:** NemoClaw/OpenShell

Then design ClawDapter so a new platform can later be added:
- Claude Code
- Codex
- others

That gives you:
- a useful concrete starting point
- no fake abstraction too early

- a clean path to future runtimes

---

# What to avoid

## Don’t do this
- hardcode OpenClaw request/response shapes into ClawDash
- scatter NemoClaw-specific checks throughout the UI
- assume Claude Code or Codex will look like OpenClaw sessions
- design around one runtime’s streaming/event format

## Do this instead
- define one internal contract
- normalize backend events/results
- let ClawTomation own routing decisions
- treat backend differences as adapter concerns

# Proposed capability: Linked Workstreams
Think of this as a ClawDash-native coordination layer between related product lanes.

For example:

- **Project:** ClawDash
- **Linked workstream:** ClawCanvas
- **Relationship:** central-blade integration
- **Shared contract:** shell ↔ canvas relationship

- **Sync rule:** route/state/handoff changes require cross-lane acknowledgement

That gives us enforced synergy instead of informal chat relay.

---

# Core model
## 1) Project manifest
Each made-for-OpenClaw product gets a machine-readable manifest describing:

- project identity
- linked projects/workstreams
- related Discord threads
- related OpenClaw agent sessions
- required contract docs
- which changes require sync

Example shape:

```yaml
project: clawdash
linked_workstreams:
  - id: clawcanvas
    relationship: central-blade-integration
    contract_docs:
      - docs/foundation/CLAWDASH_CLAWCANVAS_RELATIONSHIP.md
    sync_required_on:
      - route_change
      - shared_state_change
      - blade_handoff_change
      - package_boundary_change
      - milestone_status_change
```

## 2) Contract docs
Repo docs remain canonical for durable truth.

For this case:
- `docs/foundation/CLAWDASH_CLAWCANVAS_RELATIONSHIP.md`

This doc defines:
- ownership
- boundaries
- shared state
- handoff rules
- routing rules
- anti-drift rules

## 3) Sync packets
When one lane makes an integration-relevant change, ClawDash generates or requires a **sync packet**:

- what changed
- why
- affected files/docs
- contract impact
- action required from linked lane
- ack status

## 4) Delivery adapters
ClawDash should deliver sync packets through proper integrations:

- **primary:** OpenClaw session/agent messaging
- **secondary:** Discord notification
- **tertiary:** Memory summary

That means even if a Discord post is invisible to another agent, the linked agent still gets a real work item through the OpenClaw side.

## 5) Ack + drift tracking

A sync packet is not done until:
- linked lane receives it
- linked lane acknowledges it
- any required contract update is completed

If that doesn’t happen, ClawDash shows:
- **pending sync**
- **out-of-date linked lane**
- **contract drift risk**

## 3. Status/signal strip
Tiny ambient signals that help the workspace stay informed.

Example:
- Health: degraded service
- Cost: usage spike
- Review: blocking review pending

## 4. Quick actions

Buttons/actions that let the user jump into meaningful work.

Example:
- “Open active project”
- “Resume last memory context”
- “Review pending items”
- “Capture decision note”

## 5. Context companion
A blade can enrich the current Canvas session with context.

Example:
- Memory provides relevant notes for active project
- Docs provides referenced docs
- Projects provides current milestone context

## 6. Deep-link handoff

Every contribution must point to the full blade experience.

That keeps Canvas from turning into a giant overloaded mega-page.

---

# What blades should *not* be allowed to do
This matters just as much.

Blades should **not** be able to:
- replace the central workspace region by default
- inject arbitrary full-screen UI
- hijack navigation
- dump huge dashboards into Canvas
- redefine core page structure
- bypass shell policy

So this is an SDK for **bounded integration**, not uncontrolled plugins.

---

# Integration tiers
This gives us a clean maturity model.

## Tier 0 — Route only
Blade exists, but only as a standalone page.

## Tier 1 — Handoff
Blade gets a card/link on Canvas.

## Tier 2 — Summary
Blade contributes a small widget or signal.

## Tier 3 — Context-aware

Blade can respond to current workspace context.

## Tier 4 — Embedded workflow
Rare, explicit approval required.

Only a few blades should ever reach Tier 4.

That keeps ClawCanvas central instead of becoming a module dump.

---

## Memory
Contribute:

Full blade:

Full blade:

Full blade:

Full blade:

Full blade:

Full blade:

## Cost

Full blade:

# What this looks like in code
The pattern should be:

- **ClawCanvas defines the surfaces**
- **blades register contributions**
- **ClawCanvas chooses what to render**

So something like:

```ts
defineBlade({
```

```ts
  id: 'memory',
  kind: 'system',
  canvas: {
    handoff: {...},
    summaryWidget: {...},
    signals: [...],
    quickActions: [...],
    contextProvider: ...
  }
})
```

That means blades declare intent, but Canvas still controls layout and policy.

## Canvas registry
Inside `packages/clawcanvas`:

# The important guardrails
These should be hard rules.

## 1. Canvas owns composition
Blades contribute, Canvas composes.

## 2. Every blade contribution needs a deep-link
Home-page integrations should always lead somewhere real.

## 3. Performance budget
No heavy integrations on the home page.

## 4. Fallback-safe
If a blade fails, Canvas still works.

## 5. Tiered permissions
Not every blade gets every surface.

## 6. System blades first
Memory / Projects / Tasks / Review / Docs / Health / Cost get first-class support before broader extension.

## 7. No center-region takeover without explicit approval
The central workspace pane remains protected.

Because it solves the exact tension you called out:

# Refined integration philosophy
## Default behavior
**Surface → inform → hand off**

Not:
**surface → take over the canvas**

That means the default blade-to-canvas interaction should be:

1. a signal/notification appears in Canvas
2. it includes useful summary info
3. it offers a deep link / handoff to the blade
4. optionally it can expand into a richer embedded view
5. but that richer view is **opt-in**, not the default mode

That preserves Canvas as the lived-in home.

# Standardized blade → canvas integration methods
I think we should define a small, strict set of standardized methods.

## 1. Alert / signal
For important events.

Examples:
- PR opened on registered project repo
- deployment failed
- task overdue
- review required
- cost spike
- memory recall suggested

This should be:
- lightweight
- glanceable
- actionable
- dismissible/snoozable if appropriate

## 2. Handoff
A deep link from Canvas to the blade.

Examples:
- “Open Project”
- “Review PR context”
- “See pending review”
- “Open memory result”

This is probably the most important one.

## 3. Peek / expanded preview
Optional richer info inside Canvas.

Examples:
- PR summary with title, branch, author, status checks
- task cluster preview

- doc preview
- memory context snippet

Important:
- user-triggered or policy-triggered
- not full blade takeover
- temporary and bounded

## 4. Context contribution
A blade enriches the current Canvas session.

Examples:
- active project context
- linked docs
- related memory
- current milestone
- open PRs relevant to active work

This should help the agent work better without forcing page-switching.

## 5. Quick action
Small actions callable from Canvas.

Examples:
- create task
- open project
- attach PR to review
- capture note
- mark reminder
- start retrospective

# Important architectural consequence
If we want Watch/iPhone/iPad/Desktop/Web to all work well, then we need to avoid building Canvas as a single giant bespoke webpage.

Instead we should build:

## Core product contracts
- blade metadata
- blade events
- canvas integration payloads
- notifications
- handoffs
- quick actions
- agent context model
- deep links / universal links

## Then platform-specific renderers
Each platform decides how to present those:

### Web/Desktop
- full cards

- peeks
- side panels
- embedded previews
- richer workspace orchestration

### iPad
- split view / compact cards / stronger voice affordances

### iPhone
- feed + prompts + quick actions + handoffs

### Watch
- voice intents
- tiny summaries
- approve/dismiss/capture/remind actions

So the integration model needs to be **API-first and render-agnostic**.

# The core idea
ClawDash should have a **shared integration/action fabric** where:

- blades publish events/signals into Canvas

- Canvas surfaces them as alerts, handoffs, or previews
- the user or agent can take action from there
- those actions route to the **correct owning blade**
- blades reuse each other’s core capabilities instead of duplicating them

So a toast is not just a notification.  
It is an **entry point into the workspace action graph**.

---

# The 3 integration planes
I’d formalize this as 3 separate but connected planes:

## 1. Display plane
How a blade surfaces something into Canvas:
- toast
- alert card

- inbox item
- handoff link
- optional preview

## 2. Action plane
What the user/agent can do from that surfaced thing:
- task for later
- open in project
- attach to review
- create note
- save doc link
- ask agent to summarize
- snooze/dismiss/remind

## 3. Ownership plane
Which blade actually owns that function:
- Tasks owns tasks

- Docs owns canonical docs/files
- Projects owns project metadata and repo associations
- Review owns review objects/checkpoints
- Memory owns recallable working memory
- Health owns health signals
- Cost owns usage/cost signals

This third plane is what prevents duplication.

---

# Your PR example, properly modeled
## Event source
Projects blade knows:
- this GitHub repo belongs to Project X
- PR #184 was opened

## Canvas display
Canvas shows a toast/alert like:

- `PR #184 opened in ClawDash`
- title
- repo/project
- author/check status
- maybe short AI summary

## Actions available right there
From the toast or from the agent in Canvas:

- **Open Project**
- **Preview here**
- **Ask agent what this means**
- **Create review item**
- **Task for later**

- **Link to docs**
- **Dismiss / snooze**

## If user clicks “Task for later”
That should **not** create a fake task inside Projects or Canvas.

Instead:
- Canvas sends an action to **Tasks**
- Tasks creates a real task
- task includes backlinks to:
  - project
  - PR
  - source event
- optionally Projects records that a linked follow-up task exists

That is the pattern.

---

# This is the anti-duplication rule
## Blades may originate context
but they should call into the **owner blade** for canonical actions.

Examples:

### Projects
May surface:
- repo events
- milestones
- project status

But if user says:
- “make this a task”

then **Tasks** owns the result.

### Review
May surface:
- approval needed
- retro item
- validation checkpoint

But if user says:
- “remind me later”
- “track this follow-up”

then **Tasks** owns it.

### Memory
May surface:
- relevant recall

- prior decision
- suggested context

But if user says:
- “make this a canonical reference”

then **Docs** or **Projects** may own the durable destination depending on type.

### Docs
Owns:
- canonical documents/files

So Projects, Review, Memory should link into Docs rather than each inventing their own document storage model.

That’s exactly the system discipline you want.

---

# A good blade rule
I’d lock this:

> **A blade can present, reference, and request actions, but canonical creation/update of domain objects should be handled by the blade that owns that object type.**

That gives us clean boundaries.

---

# Agent integration is a huge part of this
And yes — because so much of this product is agent-driven, the agent on Canvas should act like a **broker** across blades.

So from Canvas you should be able to say things like:

- “task this for later”
- “attach this PR to the current project”
- “make a review item from this”

- “save this as a note”
- “link this doc to the project”
- “show me related memory”
- “remind me tomorrow”

The agent should translate that into standardized actions against the right blade.

So the agent is not just chat.  
It is a **workspace action router**.

# This suggests a better SDK shape
Not just “blade contribution SDK.”

More like:

## 1. Contribution API
For surfacing things into Canvas:
- signals
- alerts
- handoffs
- peeks
- context contributions

## 2. Action API
For operating back into blades:
- createTask
- updateProject
- createReviewItem
- attachDoc
- saveMemoryNote
- snoozeAlert
- createReminder

- summarizeEvent

## 3. Ownership registry
A registry of who owns what canonical object/capability.

- task

- follow-up state

## Projects blade owns
- project

- project status

- project metadata

## Docs blade owns

Then any blade can **invoke** another blade’s capability through the action fabric, but not reimplement it.

# Two-way and cross-blade examples
## Example 1: PR alert → task
- Projects emits PR event
- Canvas shows alert
- user taps “task for later”
- Tasks creates item
- Projects gets backlink

## Example 2: Review item → docs
- Review flags missing decision record
- action “create doc stub”
- Docs creates canonical doc entry
- Review links it

## Example 3: Memory recall → project update
- Memory finds prior decision relevant to active project
- agent says “apply this to current project summary”
- Projects updates project context
- Docs may link the canonical note

## Example 4: Docs → tasks
- Doc has unresolved TODO or action section
- agent says “turn open items into tasks”
- Tasks owns the created tasks
- Docs just remains the source

That’s the ecosystem behavior we want.

- Review later
- Save note
- Link to project
- Snooze
- Dismiss

Not every alert needs every action, but the pattern should be standardized.

Ephemeral, time-sensitive

Running list of things to check later

or

Structured actions, not ad hoc glue.

So you can always trace:
- where it came from

## Post 2 — Proposed NORTHSTAR content

### Core direction
ClawCanvas is the **central workspace page** for ClawDash: the place a builder returns to in order to work, switch context, review artifacts, and move projects forward.

### Mission
Make ClawDash’s central working surface something the owner actively wants to use for claw development because it is better than falling back to Discord or legacy control views.

### Product promise
ClawCanvas is the **central workspace blade** for ClawDash:
- function switching
- workspace/channel switching
- active conversation/work
- mixed artifacts
- adjacent working context

### Center of gravity
The center of gravity is the **active workspace surface**, not a management console.

### Strategic locks
1. **Two-axis shell lock**
   - top purpose tabs
   - lateral workspace/channel rail

2. **Compatibility-preserving transformation lock**

- preserve routing/control primitives while changing experience
   - `/chat` remains canonical unless intentionally changed later

3. **Workspace-first lock**
   - home must resolve to workspace behavior, not legacy management semantics

4. **Mixed-content lock**
   - ClawCanvas must support more than plain text chat
   - attachments, previews, documents are part of intended surface

5. **Security/trust lock**
   - preserve sanitization, safe URL handling, auth boundaries, control-ui semantics

6. **ClawDash alignment lock**
   - ClawCanvas is part of ClawDash’s product center of gravity
   - not a detached infra console

### Do-not-become
ClawCanvas must not become:
- a thin reskin of legacy control UI
- a fleet manager
- a route alias with unchanged experience
- a control page with chat bolted on
- a place where `main/master session` remains the real home concept

### Canonical experience model
A user lands in ClawCanvas and feels they are inside a **working environment**, not a maintenance surface.

Expected composition:
- top tabs for function switching
- channel rail for context switching
- central work surface anchored by conversation + artifacts
- adjacent modules for docs, memory, project context, alerts, active work support

### Canonical terms
Use:
- ClawCanvas
- central workspace
- workspace blade
- central blade
- function tabs
- channel rail
- workspace channels

Avoid:
- master page
- main/master as product identity
- display surface as primary identity
- management-surface-default framing

### Boundaries with ClawDash

ClawCanvas is for:
- central interactive work
- active project context
- mixed artifacts
- switching across current work channels/functions
- making ClawDash feel like the place to build

ClawCanvas is not primarily for:
- raw infrastructure administration
- fleet/node/instance management
- settings-heavy management posture
- making Discord the product home

### Current maturity read
**Transitional but directionally locked.**

Materially true:

- shell direction is decided
- `/clawcanvas` route work exists
- mixed-content groundwork exists

Not yet true:
- finished ClawCanvas home composition
- center pane fully matches intended vision
- full experiential break from legacy feel

### Confidence
**Medium-high**

### Assumptions
- Channel decisions from 2026-03-15 through 2026-03-16 are the active baseline
- No later ClawDash northstar supersedes this yet
- Existing compatibility constraints remain in force

### Contradictions found
- Strategy is ahead of visible experience
- Naming is ahead of implementation
- Repo ownership intent and implementation location are misaligned

### Open questions
- What exact home composition defines ClawCanvas beyond chat?
- Which legacy control elements remain first-class vs secondary?
- What is the canonical ClawDash repo/package home?
- Is `/chat` permanent or transitional?
- Which legacy copy should be retired first?

How will we make sure the two lanes stay in sync on development, I don’t want you two drifting in integration
There is the clawdash lane in <#1482948013289902202>  and this lane

We keep them in sync by making sure they **do not own different truths**.

## The rule
- **Repo docs** = canonical truth
- **This lane** = product/integration arbitration
- **ClawDash lane** = implementation execution

So the lanes are not two sources of truth.  
They are **two operating lanes around one source of truth**.

## Split of responsibility

### This lane: ClawCanvas product + integration lane
Owns:
- northstar
- v1 scope boundaries
- route/copy decisions

- shell↔canvas contract decisions
- what gets ported vs left behind
- acceptance of integration-sensitive work

### ClawDash lane: build lane
Owns:
- concrete implementation
- changed files
- build/test status
- blockers found in code
- proposed technical tradeoffs
- PR/commit-ready execution state

## What must be shared between both lanes every time
For any meaningful slice, both lanes should reference the same packet:

- `packages/clawcanvas/docs/FOUNDATION.md`

- `packages/clawcanvas/docs/NORTHSTAR.md`
- `packages/clawcanvas/docs/MILESTONES.md`
- `packages/clawcanvas/docs/AGENT_HANDOFF_2026-03-16.md`

That means if one lane says something that is not in those docs or in a current handoff/update, it is **not durable yet**.

## Anti-drift protocol

### 1. No silent decision changes
If ClawDash lane changes any of these:
- route
- naming/copy
- shell behavior
- blade boundaries
- integration contract
- v1 scope

…it has to post back here before that becomes “real”.

### 2. No silent implementation assumptions
If this lane makes a new strategic call, it is not “done” until:
- docs are updated
- ClawDash lane acknowledges the change
- implementation impact is called out

### 3. Every work slice gets a compact handoff
Required handoff format:
- scope
- files touched
- routes affected
- APIs/contracts affected
- build/test result
- blockers
- next recommended step

### 4. One lane leads, one lane reviews
For each slice:
- one lane is **driver**
- the other lane is **review/integration check**

Not both driving the same thing at once.

### 5. Integration gate before merge
Anything touching:
- app shell
- routing
- canvas home
- support-blade handoffs
- naming/copy migration

needs a quick cross-lane check before merge.

## Practical operating model

### For now, I recommend:
- **This lane** = decides product shape and integration boundaries
- **ClawDash lane** = implements Phase work and reports back

That is the cleanest way to avoid drift.

## Session-level sync rule
At the **start** of a work session:
- read the 4 canonical docs + current handoff

At the **end** of a work session:
- post a handoff delta back to the other lane

So neither lane resumes from stale assumptions.

## The real key
The important thing is **not** “keep both chats identical.”

It is:

> keep both lanes pointed at the same repo truth, with explicit handoffs and no silent contract drift.

Yes — **EXT over LVM** for this build. ✅

For your single-host 2xNVMe RAID1 setup, EXT is the better default because:

- thin provisioning behavior is better/simpler
- easier day-2 operations
- aligns with XCP-ng current recommendation patterns for local SR

So choose:

- **Software RAID1** for host disks
- **Local EXT SR** for VM storage

## Add MajorBBS Windows VM plan

You can host it on XCP-ng with no major design change. Just treat it as a separate workload tier.

### Recommended placement
- Keep **core infra** on mirrored NVMe (VyOS, XO, OpenClaw admin/prod)
- Put **Windows MajorBBS VM** on same mirrored SR initially (for reliability)
- Later, if you add bulk storage, you can move less-critical disks off fast tier

### Suggested starting VM spec (MajorBBS)
- vCPU: **2–4**
- RAM: **4–8 GB** (depends on users/doors/load)
- Disk: **80–160 GB** (start 100 GB)
- NIC: 1 (on app/prod VLAN behind VyOS)
- Boot firmware: standard (UEFI is fine unless app tooling prefers BIOS/legacy)

### Networking/security
- Don’t expose RDP publicly
- Access via VPN/Tailscale + firewall allowlist only
- If publishing BBS ports, publish only required ports via VyOS/reverse-proxy path
- Keep it logically separate from OpenClaw admin network

### Backup
- Snapshot before major updates
- Daily VM backup (off-host)
- Separate export/copy of MajorBBS data/config (in-guest backup too)

---

## What I’d improve (important)
### 1) Keep Ubuntu/boot-from-ZFS as optional, not mandatory control layer
You can absolutely host your Ubuntu VM later for app/file workloads, but don’t make it mandatory as “the” storage backbone yet.  
If it dies, you now create a **storage-control-chain failure** for OpenClaw too. For v0, run infra on XCP-ng SR directly first.

**Recommendation:**  
- v0: **Local EXT SR on XCP-ng** for all infra VMs (VyOS, admin OpenClaw, prod OpenClaw, Windows/MajorBBS).  
- v1+: optionally add Ubuntu + ZFS/SMB/NFS for non-critical/project workloads.

---

### 2) Treat “two OpenClaw instances on same host” as **control-plane + workload-plane separation**
It’s good, but in this one-host reality it’s not HA. Make that explicit in ops docs and runbooks:  
- one VM recovers the other, not “survivability through redundancy”.  
- if either VM fails, the other is expected to be the repair lane.

---

### 3) Security posture: lock management first (you just brought this home)
Since your UI is currently reachable at `147.135.105.34`, do this before big provisioning:

- allowlist SSH/HTTPS only from trusted admin source(s),
- no inbound RDP/GUI from public anywhere,
- do not expose VM services publicly yet,
- route management through VPN/Tailscale + VyOS policy rules.

---

### 4) Network shape (high-impact fix)
Use VyOS as trunked router VM and keep VM segmentation explicit:
- `mgmt`
- `admin`
- `prod`

- optional `dmz/border` (only if needed)

XCP-ng’s VLAN trunking guidance is exactly for this pattern.

---

### 5) Recovery-first: backups as architecture, not cleanup
For single-host systems, **DR is the real architecture**. You want:
- XO config + metadata backup,
- VM snapshots/backups to off-host storage,
- host metadata snapshots (pool/host export),
- tested restore path for “lost host, keep services”.

### 1) One-time setup on XCP-ng (run this locally on console)
```bash
useradd -m -s /bin/bash xcpng-bot
mkdir -p /home/xcpng-bot/.ssh
cat > /home/xcpng-bot/.ssh/authorized_keys <<'EOF'
# paste YOUR public key here (the one you want me to use)
EOF
chmod 700 /home/xcpng-bot/.ssh
```

```bash
chmod 600 /home/xcpng-bot/.ssh/authorized_keys
chown -R xcpng-bot:xcpng-bot /home/xcpng-bot/.ssh
usermod -aG wheel xcpng-bot
```

### 2) Restrict sudo to what we actually need (safer)
```bash
cat > /etc/sudoers.d/xcpng-bot <<'EOF'
xcpng-bot ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/xe
EOF
chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/xcpng-bot
visudo -cf /etc/sudoers.d/xcpng-bot
```

This gives me what I need for VM/network/bootstrap automation via `xe` without over-exposure.

is this source or destination port

Great question.

For your case, it’s mostly **destination port** for allow rules.

### Quick rule:
- **Destination port (DST port):** the service you’re trying to reach (e.g., `53`, `80`, `443`, `123`).
- **Source port (SRC port):** usually random ephemeral on the initiating host (often `32768-65535`), and you normally **don’t** need to restrict it.

### For your situation (host `147.135.105.34` egress blocked):
Allow **outbound** traffic with:

- Protocol: `ICMP` (for ping)
- Protocol: `TCP` destination ports `80, 443` (for web)
- Protocol: `UDP/TCP` destination port `53` (DNS)  
- (Optional) Protocol: `UDP` destination port `123` (NTP)

If rule UI asks for src/dst port:

- set **dest port = 80/443/53**
- leave **source port = any** (or 32768–65535 if forced)

If OVH firewall is stateful, response traffic is usually allowed automatically. If it’s stateless, you’ll need return/inbound allowances too.

### Yes, for ENF to “mean something,” use explicit **Allow + final Deny**.

And since ENF is inbound-focused, you want:

1) broad allow for trusted sources  
2) optional established traffic allowance (if UI supports it)  
3) final deny-all

### Rule 1 — Allow return/established traffic (recommended if available)
- **Action:** `ALLOW`
- **Direction:** `IN`
- **IP version:** `IPv4`
- **Protocol:** `all` (or `TCP` + `state: Established/Related` if supported)

- **Source IP:** `ANY`
- **Destination IP:** `147.135.105.34`
- **Destination port:** `Any`

### Rule 2 — Allow trusted admin source(s) all IPv4 traffic
- **Action:** `ALLOW`
- **Direction:** `IN`
- **IP version:** `IPv4`
- **Protocol:** `ALL`
- **Source IP:** `ADMIN_IP`
- **Destination IP:** `147.135.105.34`
- **Destination port:** `Any`

(Repeat Rule 2 per trusted IP: backup IP, secondary admin IP, etc.)

- Replace/precede rule 2 with specific:

- TCP 22 (SSH)
  - TCP 443 (if you use HTTPS mgmt)
  - ICMP (ping)

### Rule 4 — Final deny
- **Action:** `DENY`
- **Direction:** `IN`
- **IP version:** `IPv4`
- **Protocol:** `ALL`
- **Source IP:** `ANY`
- **Destination IP:** `147.135.105.34`
- **Destination port:** `Any`

## Clean, idempotent way to apply (run from console/root)

```bash
cp -a /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak-$(date +%F_%H%M%S)
```

```bash
# strip old block if present, then append canonical block
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
p = Path('/etc/ssh/sshd_config')
text = p.read_text()
start = '# OPENCLAW-HARDEN-BEGIN'
end = '# OPENCLAW-HARDEN-END'

block = """\n# OPENCLAW-HARDEN-BEGIN
PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
PasswordAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
AuthenticationMethods publickey
PermitEmptyPasswords no
```

```bash
PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
# Keep emergency login only; no password auth
#RootAccess yes? only via key and explicit operational decision
# OPENCLAW-HARDEN-END
"""

if start in text and end in text:
    pre = text.split(start, 1)[0]
    post = text.split(end, 1)[1]
    text = pre + block + post
else:
    text = text.rstrip() + "\n" + block

p.write_text(text + "\n")
PY
```

Short answer: copy the ISO to the XCP‑ng host, place it into an SR mount (the ISO SR if available, otherwise Local SR), run an sr-scan so XCP‑ng registers the file as a VDI, then attach it to the VM from the Web UI.

Below are two practical methods (pick the one you prefer). I include concrete commands you can paste on your workstation / the XCP‑ng host.

Prereqs
- You have SSH access to the host (example user: xcp-ng-bot) and a key.
- You have the ISO URL or the ISO on your workstation.

Method A — recommended (upload from your workstation, register into the ISO SR)
1. Download the ISO to your workstation (or skip if you already have it):
   wget -O vyos.iso "https://github.com/vyos/vyos-nightly-build/releases/download/2026.03.15-0031-rolling/vyos-2026.03.15-0031-rolling-generic-amd64.iso"

2. Copy ISO to the host (/tmp):
   scp -i /path/to/key vyos.iso xcp-ng-bot@147.135.105.34:/tmp/

3. On the host, find SR UUIDs and types so you can pick the ISO SR:
   ssh -i /path/to/key xcp-ng-bot@147.135.105.34

sudo -n /usr/bin/xe sr-list params=uuid,name-label,type

   Note the UUID for the ISO SR (type=iso). If it has a mount point it will be under /run/sr-mount/<UUID>. If the ISO SR has no mount (some SRs are special), use Local SR (type=ext).

4. Copy the ISO into the SR mount and scan:
   # Replace <SR_UUID> with the ISO SR UUID if present, otherwise use the Local SR UUID
   SR_UUID=<SR_UUID>
   SR_MOUNT="/run/sr-mount/$SR_UUID"

   # if the mount exists
   if [ -d "$SR_MOUNT" ]; then
     sudo -n /bin/cp /tmp/vyos.iso "$SR_MOUNT/vyos-installer.iso"
     sudo -n /usr/bin/xe sr-scan uuid=$SR_UUID
     sudo -n /usr/bin/xe vdi-list sr-uuid=$SR_UUID name-label --minimal
   else
     echo "SR not mounted at $SR_MOUNT — choose a different SR or use Web UI upload."
   fi

5. Refresh the XCP-ng Web UI. The ISO should be visible in the ISO/CD selector under that SR.

6. Attach ISO to VM (Web UI) and boot:
   - VM → Actions → Attach CD or Disks → choose vyos-installer.iso → set boot order to CD first → Start VM → Console.

Method B — if the UI upload is easier (no CLI)
1. Log into XCP‑ng Web UI -> Storage -> choose the ISO SR (often called "XCP‑ng Tools" or "ISO library") -> there may be an “Upload ISO” button. Use that to upload the ISO file from your workstation.

2. After upload completes, go to your VM -> Storage -> Attach CD -> select the uploaded ISO -> Boot.

If the ISO SR has no mount (common for some XCP-ng tool SRs)
- Use Local SR instead (type=ext) as we did earlier: copy ISO into /run/sr-mount/<local-SR-uuid>/ and run sr-scan. The UI sometimes does not present non-iso SRs as an ISO library; if that happens, attach the VDI to the VM via the CLI and boot (I can do that step for you if you want).

Troubleshooting notes
- If xe sr-scan returns nothing or the UI still doesn't list the ISO, verify file permission/root ownership (sudo chown root:root "$SR_MOUNT/vyos-installer.iso"; sudo chmod 644 ...).
- If the SR mount is missing, list mounts: mount | grep sr-mount or ls /run/sr-mount
- If the UI caches results, refresh the page or logout/login.

- If you prefer, create the VM first (no ISO), then tell me “VM created” and I’ll attach the ISO and boot it with the commands I used earlier.

Do you want me to run these checks for you now (I can run them over the xcp-ng-bot account), or will you run them on the host? I’ll give the exact commands (copy/paste) and what to expect.

1) Quick status checks (run on the host or I can run them)
- List SRs and types:
  - xe sr-list params=uuid,name-label,type,shared
  - What to expect: one or more SRs; type=ext (Local storage) and type=iso (XCP‑ng Tools). If nothing prints, SRs aren’t available to the pool.
- List PBDs (physical attachment of SRs to host):
  - xe pbd-list params=uuid,sr-uuid,host-uuid,device-config,currently-attached
  - Expect: a PBD per SR with currently-attached: true. If it’s false, the SR isn’t plugged to the host.
- Check SR mountpoints and files:
  - ls -la /run/sr-mount
  - df -h | grep sr-mount
  - Expect: directories for SR UUIDs (e.g. /run/sr-mount/<sr-uuid>) and the ISO/VDI files inside.
- Check XAPI task & logs (if things hang):
  - sudo cat /var/log/xensource.log | tail -n 200

- sudo cat /var/log/xapi.log | tail -n 200

2) Typical causes and fixes
- Cause: PBD is unplugged (SR exists but not attached). Fix:
  - xe pbd-plug uuid=<pbd-uuid>
  - Then: xe sr-scan uuid=<sr-uuid>
- Cause: SR mount path missing (SR not mounted in dom0). Fix:
  - Ensure the SR device (md/LVM/partition) is present (lsblk, cat /proc/mdstat)
  - If device present, replug PBD or run: xe pbd-reconfigure uuid=<pbd-uuid> (if device-config changed), then xe pbd-plug.
- Cause: SR is present but not “iso” type, so Web UI ISO chooser won’t show it as an ISO library. You can still attach the file via CLI or put the ISO into the SR that the UI expects (XCP‑ng Tools). Fix: copy the ISO to the SR mount and run xe sr-scan.
- Cause: UI context (Pool -> Storage greyed) — you might be viewing a pool with no SRs visible to pool. Try switching to Host view (Host -> Storage) or refresh the UI after sr-scan.

3) Safe, non-destructive commands to run now (I can run them)
- Show SRs:
  - sudo -n /usr/bin/xe sr-list params=uuid,name-label,type,shared
- Show PBDs:
  - sudo -n /usr/bin/xe pbd-list params=uuid,sr-uuid,host-uuid,currently-attached,device-config

- Check mount points:
  - ls -la /run/sr-mount || echo \"no sr mounts\"
  - ls -lh /run/sr-mount/* 2>/dev/null | head -30
- SR scan (if you just copied ISOs):
  - sudo -n /usr/bin/xe sr-scan uuid=<sr-uuid>

4) If an SR is missing or corrupted
- Don’t run destructive commands (vdi-destroy, sr-forget) until we confirm.
- If the correct block device is missing (md devices not assembled), confirm RAID/mdadm:
  - cat /proc/mdstat
  - sudo mdadm --detail --scan
- If SR was accidentally removed from XAPI, we can re-create or re-register the SR with:
  - xe sr-create name-label=<label> type=ext device-config:device=/dev/<device> content-type=user
  - (This is destructive if you point at the wrong device; only do if you know the device and it contains the SR)

- The XCP-ng Tools (ISO SR) is the SR the Web UI expects for ISO files; its PBD points to /opt/xensource/packages/iso.
- The ISO currently sits in /tmp (and we previously copied it into the Local SR), so the UI's ISO browser (which uses the ISO SR path) will not list it until the ISO is placed in the ISO SR (or properly registered as a VDI in whichever SR you want to use).
- We previously registered an ISO in the Local SR — that shows up to CLI as a VDI, but the Web UI ISO selection will prefer the ISO SR and/or cache; easiest is to put the ISO where the UI expects it.

Two clean options (pick one)

Option A — Recommended: place ISO in the ISO SR (XCP-ng Tools) so it appears in the Web UI
- Pros: UI-visible, simple to attach via web console
- Cons: requires writing to /opt/xensource/packages/iso (root) — safe here

Commands to run on the host (paste as one block). This will copy the ISO to the ISO SR, set ownership, rescan the SR, then list the new VDI:

```bash
# run as a user with sudo (or I can run it via xcp-ng-bot if you want)
sudo -i

# variables (already discovered on host)
ISO_SRC="/tmp/vyos-2026.03.15-rolling-amd64.iso"
ISO_DEST="/opt/xensource/packages/iso/vyos-2026.03.15-rolling-amd64.iso"
ISO_SR_UUID="8d4d447d-12fc-9895-60f5-39d94044f23e"
```

```bash

# 1) copy into the ISO SR location
cp "$ISO_SRC" "$ISO_DEST"

# 2) make sure ownership/perm allow XAPI to see it
chown root:root "$ISO_DEST"
chmod 644 "$ISO_DEST"

# 3) ask XAPI to rescan the SR so it creates the VDI metadata
/usr/bin/xe sr-scan uuid=$ISO_SR_UUID

# 4) show the VDI created (you should see vyos listed)
/usr/bin/xe vdi-list sr-uuid=$ISO_SR_UUID params=uuid,name-label,virtual-size
```

After this, refresh the Web UI (F5). The ISO should appear in the CD/ISO picker.

Option B — Quick CLI attach (skip Web UI)
- Pros: no need to put file into ISO SR; I can attach the ISO VDI to your VM via CLI and boot
- Cons: not visible in UI ISO picker; uses Local SR (we already have a VDI there)

If you already created the VM and want me to finish it for you, I can:
- Create a CD VDI in Local SR, write the ISO to it, attach it to the VM, set boot order to CD first, and start the VM.
Tell me:
- VM UUID or name (if created), or say “I created vyos-router-v1” and I’ll find it
- If you want me to run the Option A commands or proceed with Option B (CLI attach + boot)

Extra notes about the storage tab being greyed out
- The Pool-level Storage page in the UI often shows only SRs that are *pool-shared*; local SRs are not shared and are more visible from the Host view. If you want to manage Local storage, switch to Host → Storage in the Web UI (select the host, then Storage) or use the CLI `xe sr-list` we ran.

Which do you want me to do now?
- “COPY TO ISO SR” — I’ll run Option A and register the ISO for you (I can run it now with the xcp-ng-bot sudoers we set up).
- “ATTACH TO VM” — I’ll attach the ISO from Local SR to your existing VM and boot it.
- Or you can run the Option A block yourself on the host and tell me when done.
i forgot to make a user account for myself and now i cant ssh in as root, i'll need to console in, what are the steps to get the ssh reconfigured to let me ssh in and setup my account

Do everything from the host console (XCP‑ng web UI console, IPMI/iLO, or physical KVM). Don’t try to re-enable remote root password login — create a key‑based admin user from the console and use that to SSH in. Steps below are safe, minimal, and follow your existing key‑only policy.

Replace USERNAME with the account name you want and PUBKEY with the public key text you will use.

1) Open the host console and log in as root
- XCP‑ng Web UI → VMs/Host → Console, or IPMI / physical KVM.
- Login at the prompt as root.

2) Create your user and give it a shell/home
- Create the account and a home directory:
  useradd -m -s /bin/bash USERNAME
- (If your distro uses adduser you can run: adduser USERNAME)

3) Add your SSH public key
- Create .ssh, set permissions, write key:

  mkdir -p /home/USERNAME/.ssh

chmod 700 /home/USERNAME/.ssh
  cat > /home/USERNAME/.ssh/authorized_keys <<'EOF'
  (paste your PUBKEY here — one line, e.g. ssh-ed25519 AAAA...)
  EOF
  chmod 600 /home/USERNAME/.ssh/authorized_keys
  chown -R USERNAME:USERNAME /home/USERNAME/.ssh

4) Give the user sudo (least privilege recommended)
Option A — Minimal (recommended for host operations you need):
- Allow only the xe tool (same pattern as xcp-ng-bot):
  echo "USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/xe" > /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME-xe
  chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME-xe

Option B — Full admin (if you want broader sudo while you finish setup):
  echo "USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" > /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME
  chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME

(When finished with setup, tighten this so only the exact commands are allowed.)

5) Ensure SSH server allows public key auth (don’t enable password auth)
- Open /etc/ssh/sshd_config in an editor (vi/nano) and confirm these lines:

  PubkeyAuthentication yes
  PasswordAuthentication no
  PermitEmptyPasswords no
  ChallengeResponseAuthentication no

- To allow key-only root only (not recommended as primary): PermitRootLogin prohibit-password (this allows root via keys but not via password). Safer: keep root locked and use your sudo user.

6) Test the SSH server config then restart sshd
- Syntax check and restart:

  sshd -t && systemctl restart sshd

- If sshd -t returns nothing (no output) it’s OK.

7) Test SSH from your workstation
- From your workstation (use your private key for the pubkey you added):

  ssh -i /path/to/your_private_key USERNAME@147.135.105.34

8) (Optional) If you need temporary root SSH key access
- If you absolutely must SSH in as root with an emergency key, copy that public key into /root/.ssh/authorized_keys (same procedure as step 3) and ensure sshd_config has PermitRootLogin prohibit-password so only keys work for root. Do not enable PasswordAuthentication for root.

9) Cleanup / harden after you’re in
- Remove any broad NOPASSWD entries once you’ve finished:
  rm /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME
- Replace with a tight sudoers line or leave xe-only rule as appropriate.
- Confirm firewall/OVH ENF still restricts SSH to allowlisted IPs.
- Make sure root has no password or locked: passwd -l root

Useful quick-check commands (console or after you SSH in):
- Verify user and groups: id USERNAME
- Verify authorized key: sudo -u USERNAME cat ~USERNAME/.ssh/authorized_keys
- Check sshd status: systemctl status sshd
- Test sshd config: sshd -t
- See listening ssh port: ss -tlnp | grep sshd

If you want, tell me:
- The PUBLIC KEY string you want added (I can add it from the console if you prefer), or
- Confirm you will paste it on console and I’ll wait while you test.

I can also:
- Add the user and key for you if you give me the public key and confirm which sudo scope you want (xe-only or full).

im on the xcp console, but copying and pasting doesn't work. if we can enable passworded login for the new user, add my new user to the admin group, we should be able to get back to ssh only login quickly

Good — here’s a safe, minimal procedure you can run from the host console to create a local user, enable password login only for that user, give them admin rights, test SSH, and then harden again.

Important security notes before you run anything:
- This enables password auth temporarily *only for the new user* (we’ll do that using an sshd Match block). That’s safer than enabling PasswordAuthentication globally.
- Choose a strong password. Revert to key-only auth ASAP once you have your account set up.
- Because you’re on the console, paste the commands exactly, replacing USERNAME and the password prompt content when asked.

Commands to run on the host console (run as root):

1) Create the user and set a password
Replace USERNAME with the account name you want.

useradd -m -s /bin/bash USERNAME
passwd USERNAME
# follow prompts to set a strong password

2) Add the user to the admin group (wheel on XCP-ng) so sudo works

usermod -aG wheel USERNAME

3) Create a scoped sudo rule (optional but recommended) — allow only xe commands without a password
cat > /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME-xe <<'EOF'
USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/xe
EOF
chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME-xe

(If you instead want full sudo for setup, use:
echo "USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" > /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME
and chmod 440 that file — but remove it when finished.)

4) Configure sshd so only the new user can auth with a password
- Make a safe backup first:

cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /root/sshd_config.bak

- Ensure global PasswordAuthentication is disabled, then add a Match block to allow password only for the new user (replace USERNAME):

# disable global password auth
sed -i 's/^#\?PasswordAuthentication .*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# ensure root cannot login with password
sed -i 's/^#\?PermitRootLogin .*/PermitRootLogin prohibit-password/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# make sure pubkey auth remains enabled
sed -i 's/^#\?PubkeyAuthentication .*/PubkeyAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# append a Match block to allow password auth for the new user only
cat >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config <<EOF

# Allow password authentication only for the specified emergency user
Match User USERNAME
  PasswordAuthentication yes

EOF

- Validate sshd config and restart SSH:

sshd -t && systemctl restart sshd

If sshd -t returns nothing (silent), config is valid. If it reports errors, restore the backup:
cp /root/sshd_config.bak /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd

5) Test SSH from your workstation
From your laptop:
ssh USERNAME@147.135.105.34
# you should be prompted for the password you set

6) After you confirm SSH works, do any setup (upload your key, configure sudo, etc.) then harden:
- Upload your public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys for USERNAME (from the console or via SSH once logged in).

- Remove temporary password allowance when done:

# remove the Match block (or edit it out) and set global PasswordAuthentication no
sed -i '/^Match User USERNAME/,/^$/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sed -i 's/^#\?PasswordAuthentication .*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd

- Optionally remove the broad sudoers line and replace it with a narrower one (e.g., xe-only), or remove the file if you created full NOPASSWD.

7) Lock root (recommended)
passwd -l root

---

If you want, I can:
- Add your public key and create the user for you now (you’d paste the public key here), or
- Walk you through the console steps interactively.
Which do you prefer?

im starting to think maybe proxmox would have been a better idea. one of my friends told me he liked it a lot better and im starting to see some of his points, lets revise our plans so we reinstall our host OS as proxmox with ZFS
additionally, nvidia just dropped nemoclaw, and i want to make sure clawdash works on that, so we'll have 3 systems
1 nemoclaw sysadmin instance
1 openclaw production/dev instance
1 nemoclaw proudction/dev instance

Good call to re-evaluate — Proxmox + ZFS is a solid choice for the “walk” stage. It’s more turnkey for ZFS root, has nicer storage/backup integrations (Proxmox + Proxmox Backup Server), and its UI and tooling are comfortable for single‑host ops. Below I’ll give a short recommendation, pros/cons vs your current XCP-ng/mdadm approach, practical plan to move, and important items for the Nemoclaw/GPU requirements.

TL;DR
- Proxmox + ZFS (mirror of the two NVMe drives) is a good fit for a single-host, rebuild-first workflow. It simplifies management, snapshots, and backups.
- Keep your separation of concerns: a VyOS/Vyatta VM (router), a sys-admin OpenClaw VM, an OpenClaw prod/dev VM, and a Nemoclaw VM for GPU workloads.
- Critical: if you expect running Nemoclaw GPU workloads concurrently across VMs, a single GPU is a hard limit — passthrough gives one VM exclusive access. For multiple Nemoclaw VMs you need extra GPUs or NVIDIA vGPU (license + compatible hardware).
- I can provide an exact reinstall and migration plan and the commands when you say “go”.

1) High-level pros / cons: Proxmox+ZFS vs current XCP-ng + mdadm
- Proxmox + ZFS — pros
  - ZFS root installer supported out-of-the-box; easy ZFS dataset management.
  - Native snapshotting, rollback, thin clones, fast QCOW/ZVOL management.
  - Tight integration with Proxmox Backup Server (deduped, chunked backups).
  - Friendly UI for attaching ISOs, creating VMs, configuring PCI passthrough.
  - Good community docs and easy non-subscription use (just disable enterprise repo).
- Proxmox + ZFS — cons / things to watch
  - ZFS is memory hungry: reserve ~1 GB ARC per 1 TB of storage nominally (practical guidance: give ZFS headroom; for 1 TB mirror, small machines fine but keep RAM in mind).

- ZFS tuning matters (ashift, compression, no dedupe unless you have lots of RAM).
  - If you want a separate host for backups or offhost replication, plan that (PBS or another host).
- XCP-ng + mdadm — pros
  - Simpler, lower memory overhead than ZFS ARC.
  - If you already invested in XCP-ng tooling and automation, switching has a migration cost.
  - Better if you want minimal RAM consumption and simpler SR handling.
- XCP-ng + mdadm — cons
  - More manual storage management, less integrated snapshot/backup tooling.
  - UI/ISO handling quirks we ran into can be annoying.

2) Recommendations for a Proxmox + ZFS single-host topology
- Install Proxmox VE using the installer’s “ZFS RAID1” option to get rpool on ZFS mirror (use ashift=12 for NVMe/4K drives).
- Use ZFS dataset layout:
  - rpool (root)
  - rpool/data — child dataset for VM disks (Proxmox will manage qemu/vm-<id>.raw or ZVOLs)
  - Use ZVOLs for VM block devices (raw) or use qcow2 on datasets, but prefer raw/ZVOL for performance.
  - Compression: enable lz4 (low CPU overhead, good compression on many workloads).

- Dedupe: do NOT enable unless you understand memory cost (dedupe needs a lot of RAM).
- SR layout: one local ZFS pool (mirror). For backups/immutable storage, run Proxmox Backup Server (PBS) offhost (cloud or another machine). For 3-2-1 backup policy, keep one offhost copy.
- Proxmox subscription: the free repo works fine; disable enterprise repo and enable the no-subscription repo.

3) GPU / Nemoclaw considerations
- PCIe passthrough (vfio) is supported in Proxmox. Requirements:
  - Enable IOMMU in BIOS (VT-d for Intel, AMD-Vi for AMD).
  - If your CPU supports ACS and the motherboard groups permit isolation, passthrough is simpler; sometimes you need ACS override — avoid unless understood.
  - Blacklist the host NVIDIA driver so the GPU can be bound to vfio-pci.
  - Bind GPU to vfio-pci before starting the VM (Proxmox UI can do this or use /etc/modprobe.d).
  - If you want to run Nemoclaw (NVIDIA inference tooling) in a VM you will need to install the proper NVIDIA drivers in the VM and use passthrough.
- Limitations:
  - Single discrete GPU can only be assigned to one VM at a time (unless using vGPU licensing).
  - If you want two Nemoclaw VMs running concurrently you need a multi-GPU host (or vGPU license + supported GPU/hypervisor).
  - For CUDA workloads, tune hugepages and isolate CPU/memory for performance.
- Test hardware passthrough early (before moving lots of VMs) — GPU passthrough is the biggest practical gotcha.

4) VM sizing suggestions (starting point; tune to workload)
- Nemoclaw (prod/dev) VM — GPU-enabled:
  - vCPUs: 8 (or map to physical cores)
  - RAM: 32–64 GB (GPU workloads like large models need a lot of host RAM for shuffle/context)
  - Disk: 100+ GB on NVMe ZVOL for model data
- OpenClaw (prod/dev):
  - vCPUs: 4–8
  - RAM: 8–16 GB
  - Disk: 50–100 GB
- Nemoclaw sysadmin (management):
  - vCPUs: 2–4
  - RAM: 4–8 GB
  - Disk: 32–50 GB
- All VMs should have their system disks on ZVOLs/datasets and their persistent data on separate datasets for easy snapshotting/backup.

5) Backups & DR
- Use Proxmox Backup Server (PBS) for fast, deduped, incremental backups (works very well with ZFS source).

- Keep offhost backups (object storage, remote PBS) with immutability if possible.
- Snapshot and test restore procedure: test at least once before migrating production.
- Document and store the PSKs / PBS encryption keys securely.
- Keep Xen Orchestra equivalent: Proxmox + PBS + snapshots covers most needs.

6) Migration & install plan (high level, single-host)
1. Inventory: list VMs, current disk usage, network config, NIC VLANs, important IPs.
2. Backup everything (export VMs, download ISOs, config files). Keep offhost copies.
3. Prepare recovery media (Proxmox installer USB).
4. Reinstall host with Proxmox + ZFS mirror root (installer option). Use ashift=12.
5. Post-install tuning:
   - Update apt sources (no-subscription repo if you want free)
   - Install any needed kernel modules, enable IOMMU in grub:
     - For AMD: add `amd_iommu=on iommu=pt` to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT
     - For Intel: `intel_iommu=on iommu=pt`
   - Update /etc/default/grub and run `update-grub` then reboot.
6. Recreate networks in Proxmox (bridges for mgmt/admin/prod).

7. Upload ISO images to the Proxmox ISO storage (via GUI or `scp` to /var/lib/vz/template/iso).
8. Create VMs: VyOS first (router), then sysadmin OpenClaw, then OpenClaw prod/dev, then Nemoclaw VM with GPU passthrough.
9. Install guest OSes, configure networking, test inter-VM connectivity (router rules).
10. Configure PBS and schedule backups. Send first full backup offhost.
11. Test GPU passthrough on Nemoclaw VM: ensure driver install and Nemoclaw stack works.
12. Hardening: firewall rules, allowlist admin IPs, SSH key-only for users, backup verification, immutable retention, monitoring.

7) Proxmox/ZFS tuning notes (practical)
- Use ashift=12 for NVMe (4K sector): `zpool create -o ashift=12 rpool mirror /dev/nvme0n1 /dev/nvme1n1`
- Enable compression=lz4 on datasets: `zfs set compression=lz4 rpool/data`
- Don’t enable dedup unless you have RAM and measured benefit.
- Consider `zfs set reservation` for important ZVOLs if you want to guarantee space.
- Avoid `sync=always` on ZFS unless you need strict integrity — let VM/image fs handle fsync; guests should use write barriers appropriately.
- Use raw (not qcow2) for performance with ZVOLs unless you need snapshot QCOW features.

8) Networking and segmentation (follow what you already planned)
- Bridges: vm-net-mgmt, vm-net-admin, vm-net-prod

- VyOS VM acts as the router/firewall between vm-net-admin and vm-net-prod
- Keep hypervisor mgmt network (Proxmox web UI) on a separate management VLAN/IP and block public exposure (allowlist only)
- Use Proxmox firewall for an extra layer per-VM and per-bridge if desired

9) Testing / acceptance
- Before migration, run a PoC:
  - Install Proxmox on a spare disk or in a temporary VM (if possible) and test GPU passthrough.
  - Validate Nemoclaw on the VM — driver + Docker / container stack.
  - Validate PBS backup and restore.

10) Next steps I can help with
- Produce exact Proxmox install sequence with commands (GRUB settings, IOMMU, vfio binding, blacklist, GPU passthrough steps).
- Produce ZFS creation commands tuned to your NVMe drives (mirror, ashift).
- Create exact VM templates for VyOS, OpenClaw and Nemoclaw including cloud-init or unattended install snippets.
- Help migrate current VMs: export/import plan, or live convert VMs (if needed).
- Validate Nemoclaw requirements and give GPU passthrough checklist for your exact GPU model.

Which do you want next?
- “Plan + commands” — I’ll give exact installer and post-install commands to run (safe, copy/paste).
- “Do a PoC” — we can test GPU passthrough and Nemoclaw in a small VM first.
- “Full migration runbook” — a step-by-step checklist you can follow when you have the maintenance window.

If you want me to generate the commands now, tell me:
- Confirm you want Proxmox VE installed on the two NVMe drives as a ZFS mirror (ashift=12), and
- Which GPU model (exact vendor/model) you have for Nemoclaw so I can tailor the passthrough instructions.

Nice — Proxmox + ZFS is a very good fit for the “walk” stage on that hardware (5900X, 128 GiB). Below is a compact, practical plan you can act on now: what to install, how to configure ZFS for NVMe mirror, the VM/network layout, recommended VM sizing for your three systems, backup/restore guidance, hardening, and follow-up options (scripts or step‑by‑step).  

If you want me to produce a one-shot install script + post‑install commands I can do that next.

1) Why Proxmox + ZFS is a good match here
- Built‑in ZFS root installer makes a stable, recoverable host with snapshots and easy dataset management.  
- Proxmox UI + PBS (Proxmox Backup Server) gives clean VM snapshot/backup workflow.  
- ZFS features (snapshots, clones, compression) speed dev/restore workflows for VM images.  
- Your CPU + 128 GiB RAM is excellent for ZFS; you’ll have room for ARC and VMs.

2) High‑level decisions to accept now
- ZFS mirror of the two NVMe drives for rpool (root + VM storage). Use ashift=12 for NVMe.
- Compression = lz4, atime = off, xattr=sa, acltype=posixacl.
- Use ZVOLs for VM block devices (Proxmox will manage these).
- No dedup (memory cost too high).
- Proxmox no-subscription repo is fine for home/small infra.

3) Quick install summary (manual or follow installer)
- Boot Proxmox VE installer USB. Choose “ZFS RAID1” and pick both NVMe devices; ensure ashift=12 if prompted or choose Advanced to set it.
- Complete install, reboot.

If you want the explicit zpool commands (manual install path):
- Example (run on installer/host when preparing disks):
  - zpool create -o ashift=12 -O compression=lz4 -O atime=off -O xattr=sa -O acltype=posixacl rpool mirror /dev/nvme0n1 /dev/nvme1n1

4) Post‑install tuning for ZFS on Proxmox
- Limit ARC so ZFS doesn’t hoard all RAM. With 128 GiB, target ARC 32–48 GiB:
  - echo "options zfs zfs_arc_max=34359738368" > /etc/modprobe.d/zfs.conf  (32 GiB)
  - update initramfs if required; reboot to apply.
- Ensure dataset compression:
  - zfs set compression=lz4 rpool
- Recommended dataset properties for VM data (Proxmox handles ZVOL creation):
  - keep default volblocksize (Proxmox uses reasonable defaults). If tuning, ZVOL volblocksize=16K is common for VM workloads.
- Do NOT enable dedup unless you understand memory needs.

5) Storage layout / names
- rpool (root and VM storage) as mirror of NVMe
- Let Proxmox create VM storage under rpool (ZVOLs)
- ISO store: copy ISOs to /var/lib/vz/template/iso or the Proxmox GUI upload
- Backups: use separate server or PBS (recommended). Offhost: cloud S3 or remote PBS.

6) Networking plan (matches your earlier plan)
- Bridges:
  - vmbr0 = mgmt/public (physical NIC trunk to internet; restrict firewall/OVH ENF)
  - vmbr1 = admin / system-admin OpenClaw
  - vmbr2 = prod / production OpenClaw + other workload networks
- Build VyOS VM first and place its NIC0 on vmbr0 (public) and NIC1 on vmbr1 and NIC2 on vmbr2 as needed; use it for routing, NAT, firewall between admin/prod.

7) VM naming & recommended resource sizing (starting points)
- vyos-router (virtualized) — small:
  - 2 vCPU, 2 GiB RAM, 8 GiB disk
- openclaw-system-admin:

- 2–4 vCPU, 8–16 GiB RAM, 50–100 GiB disk
- openclaw-production / dev:
  - 4–8 vCPU, 16–32 GiB RAM, 100+ GiB disk
- nemoclaw (if later needed for GPU): skip for now (you said no GPU). If you later add GPU, boost RAM and pin CPUs for best performance.

8) Backups & snapshot strategy
- Use Proxmox Backup Server (PBS) or native Proxmox backups to remote location.
- Retention example: daily incremental (7 days), weekly full (4 weeks), monthly offsite (3 months).
- 3‑2‑1 rule: keep on‑host snapshot, local backups on PBS, and one offsite copy (S3 / removable drive / remote PBS).
- Test restores regularly.

9) Security / hardening
- Proxmox Web UI: restrict access to allowlisted admin IPs (OVH ENF already inbound‑only).
- Enable 2FA for the Proxmox UI (TOTP).
- Create non-root admin user and use sudo; disable root password login over SSH (keep root locked).
- SSH: key-only for admin users.
- Keep updates current, subscribe to security mailing or check apt updates daily.

- Use Proxmox firewall per-VM and per-bridge where appropriate.

10) Migration path from your current XCP-ng setup
- Options:
  - Export VM disks and images (VDI → raw/OVA) → qemu-img convert to qcow2/raw → upload to Proxmox storage and create VM with existing disk.
  - Or rebuild VMs in Proxmox and migrate application data (cleanest).
- I can produce exact commands to convert an XCP‑ng VDI/VDI file to a Proxmox‑friendly format if you tell me where current VM disks are stored.

11) Management / monitoring / logging
- Add node_exporter + pve_exporter to collect metrics; Grafana for dashboards.
- ZFS health: check `zpool status` daily via cron/monitoring.
- Backup logs: monitor PBS/backup failures.

12) Useful commands (post install)
- Add Proxmox no-subscription repo:
  - echo "deb http://download.proxmox.com/debian/pve bullseye pve-no-subscription" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-no-subscription.list
  - apt update && apt full-upgrade

- Upload ISO to Proxmox:
  - scp -P 22 vyos.iso root@proxmox-host:/var/lib/vz/template/iso/
- Create VM image (using GUI recommended) or with qm CLI:
  - qm create 100 --name vyos-router-v1 --memory 2048 --net0 virtio,bridge=vmbr0
  - qm importdisk 100 vyos.iso local-lvm
  - qm set 100 --scsihw virtio-scsi-pci --scsi0 local-lvm:vm-100-disk-0
  - qm set 100 --boot order=scsi0
- ZFS check:
  - zpool status -v
  - zfs list -o name,used,avail,mountpoint

13) Suggested first actions for your maintenance window
- Make a current backup of anything important offhost.
- Create Proxmox installer USB.
- Reinstall host to Proxmox with ZFS mirror (installer option).
- Install Proxmox, update, add no-subscription repo.
- Create bridges (vmbr0/1/2) in GUI.

- Upload VyOS ISO and create the vyos-router VM (start it and configure).
- Create system-admin OpenClaw, then production OpenClaw VMs.
- Configure PBS or another backup target and run first backups.

14) I can do next steps for you
